item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting concept, I like it

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this SCP! Read it last year and read it again even though I remebered it mostly but its just so good! I hope you add onto this narritive(there might be a few tales i havent seen yet about this place but Im not concerning myself with those). I am almost at scp-3000! reading 2922 scp articles in chronological order is a lot more strenous than I orignally thought…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really do not like this. The Foundation behaves incredibly stupidly (and continues to do so judging by the expanded stuff, this isn't just a one off cockup this is institutional incompetence) and the exploring character feels far too "look how cool she is fighting against this strawman organisation I created and making friends with all the cool magic guys",

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You don't just need an ouija board to communicate with the dead anymore.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Addendum 1 is listed twice. Is it improper numbering or do these 2 documents belong to one addendum?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is absolutly one of my favorite scp articles. i love the surrealism of the locations and inhabidents of the afterlife!
i saw that you were up for collaboration for additions to the article. if this article isnt dead, i would love to give some ideas for additional inhabidents/items/locations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this. At first it seemed like it might go in a Limetown direction, then like it might be another one of the "the afterlife guardian is just another goofball" articles. The fact that the Foundation chose to do a pointlessly dickish thing was kind of dumb, but I really enjoyed the little speech (if more in idea rather than execution) at the end. Happily +1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most of this is a great twist into really unexpected territory, but I think the Foundation's repeated refusal to let her speak to her husband is stretching believeability a bit- it feels like a bit of a contrived way to drive a wedge between them when it makes the most sense for the Foundation to just let them do it. It's a big enough issue that it goes beyond just simple nitpicky plot-hole-ness from me.

But the rest was great, and a really clever setup. Overall it's an excellent article, so a clear +1 :).

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent edit by Qwags was reverted, as it incorrectly introduced a url relating to a page in the "deleted:" category.

Such pages are considered improperly deleted, and per the Deletion Guidelines, are routinely removed by staff and considered summary deletions. Editors, please refrain from linking to any pages with "deleted:" in the title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are several little problems with this.

First, there are several good skips where the true focus isn't the skip itself, but this went a little far. The title phenomenon receives too little attention and the article quickly wanders to investigation on other things, the memetic phone-call barely being more than their communicator. It would be better to break the article into a simple little skip and a log or tale with the bulk of the Afterlife Exploring section.

Second, the shit with her husband was really forced. There is no build-up in the tension, or to her stress on being cut from family. She kind of just repeats the phrase. There is no reason whatsoever for the Foundation to not bring her husband. If she worried about he never getting a normal life again and tried to convince them to take her husband, let him talk to her, and then let him go scot free, it would make some sense. But she literally asked for them to hire him. She doesn't give a fuck to his life outside their relationship, and there is no reason for the Foundation to refuse hiring someone with any kind of degree to keep a skip compliant. Heck, he would eventually be of use somewhere, there are always skips where you'll be a valuable asset with an arts degree.

When they killed her husband for a phone they couldn't even know that had anything of value, that was just random. Like changing the Monthly Termination of D-Classes to the Monthly Termination of Employee Widows. They literally killed the guy for no reason, in a fashion that probably costs more than hiring him, or amnesticizing him, and for random luck got hold of a message that wasn't nearly as valuable as the skip itself would be if they had hired the guy they killed.

Downvote, as much as it have several separate elements that could make for a good story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two plot problems that keep me from an upvote.

I find it hard to believe that the Foundation would refuse to let Janet speak to her husband. Given the presence of amnesiacs in the foundationverse, there's no reason they couldn't just let them talk and then force-feed him a few forget-me-pills. Or injections. Or whatever. If I could tell from her dialogue that she was going to turn on them if they didn't cooperate, they'd probably figure it out as well.

Her off-screen befriending of Spidertron the Impenetrable and subsequent (rather easy) bamboozling of the Foundation felt really cheap to me. Not so much that it happened in the first place, but that we almost literally jump cut from her wandering the desert to her being the right-hand-woman of an eldritch monster lord.

You've built a really good world here, but the story set inside of it just doesn't click with me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to jump on the bandwagon too. I thought that all of this was really well written and thought out, and could be because this is a revised version and far from the original. But I generally disagree with those that are saying it's grammar or anything relating to language is off.

I actually like the idea that the afterlife can be enjoyable for everyone depending on how you live your life and how you reflect upon everything when it's all said and done. I think it also helps that this reads very much like a Holder story (some of you say Tales) but I could see some not liking that.

The only complaint and nitpick I can come up with is her reactions in realizing her situation. The grieving was well written actually, but as a Foundation employee, I find it hard to believe that she'd be so surprised about some of her realizations (ie. hunger or fatigue). I feel like she'd be much more analytical (except her shock, again) and the tone or something seems off there as well.

Oh and there are two Addendum 1s. Am I not understanding that? I also feel the first shouldn't have the Project Corbenic on it seeing as it was the catalyst that started it and not part of Protect Corbenic itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I liked the dialogue and thought it was good (though I suppose this is long after it was reworked). Not even the melodrama bothers me, because I know from experience that people can get really melodramatic and soppy when it's about love or life and death. Also, I'm a sucker for love stories. Don't mind the story-SCP either, I think it's a nice format and adds to the variety.

I wanted to upvote, but my big beef with this was: the decision to not let the Spiegels talk to each other. It just seemed really dumb and short-sighted, too much so for it to have been taken by the O5-Council. I mean, by know, they ought to know, both from history and experience, that such a stance is only going to result in a total fiasco, which is exactly what they received here. It just really breaks my suspension of disbelief, and is the main reason it choose to novote. It's a big story-breaker for me.

I'd find it much more believable if the decision was made not by the O5, but by somebody else related to Project Corbenic, like this Dr. Henderson. He was the head of the project, right? I mean, it's not uncommon of people to feel that they don't wish to bother their busy superiors with emotional/moral nonsense and simple call the decisions themselves. And there have been enough examples of gross incompetence within Foundation ranks to justify that (thinking of the ghost hitchhiker skip). I'd be much more content if this is how it had went down, and Henderson had received some sort of punishment, like demotion, from his superiors, because ruining a project in such a silly manner would definitely be grounds for that.
Well, +101 now. Great skip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is honestly one of my favourite skips. The framing story baits you into thinking one thing and then promptly roundhouse kicks you into an exploration epic about a woman and her journey into not one, not two, but literally every afterlife imaginable, and the cosmic explorations thereof. Giant monkeys trying to be friends by squishing you underfoot is adorable, while the three moons motif is both alien and memorable in the best way. Even the occasionally melodramatic line feels more like a well-earned one-liner with the scale you give through these phone calls, and "I'll be watching. Win or lose, I love you forever" is a hella sweet line to end on.

Really love this, and I'm looking forward to writing more in the Corbenic setting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry I have to buck the trend. The writing tone feels off,for one. Part of me thinks English is not native for the author which could contribute. Also it feels just a little… Fantasy that everyone goes to another dimension and can never die but there are beings so casually referred to as his and nobody can die. There's very little mystery or horror, or even creepiness to me. It reads like a clinical YA fantasy adventure novel to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gave me some tingles down my spine. You get a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for referencing 2178, I love that article.

The dialogue is still a bit off in some places, and I think it could be revised more, but overall I really like the concept and it's fleshed out well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a change of pace for the stuff I'm used to seeing from you, Dave. Not gonna lie, I don't usually like the minimalist-and-silly you usually go for, but here, it feels like the minimalism works. +1 for the many, many, many unanswered questions I'll be sitting on for a long while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, this was nice. Really liked the story. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for you explaination!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because:

1. The Foundation had detected an SCP-2922-B signal coming to Mr. Spiegel, and feared that Mr. Spiegel might make top secret afterlife-related information public after receiving it from his deceased wife.

2. Though it's not directly stated in the article, MTF Omicron-0 is an assassin task force for clandestine operations, when there's simply no time to take a moral high ground and you need military action in a manner of minutes, rather than hours. If you send Omicron-0 to get you coffee, they'll come back with blood on their hands as well as coffee. Killing's their specialty.

And…

3. You said it yourself, they found a middle ground. She wants to talk to her husband? Send him to the same place!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Chinese. I'm the translator of CN breach. That's why I have to read the whole work.

But we(people in CN, after reading your article) have a question: Why refused her and killed her husband?

It's really hard to understand the reason. Just because the O5 hates to be thearted?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said upthread, I am not sure if "inoculate" is wrong. If it is as in immunology, then yes. But it could be as in "inoculum". Is language a virus and a person infected? Or is a person more like a petri dish for growing cultures of culture?

Though, the best verbs for meme transmission are probably those like "teach" and "learn".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah okay. It's all good! What's your native language if you don't mind me asking?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At a glance, this was fixed up sufficiently for me to upvote.
Up one it goes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry…
I just want to say the Foundation in here is unusually cruel, and I personly like this kind of story.A super organization does the bad thing for thier own 'great good', which makes the innocent people suffer. Cruel, but awesome.

The foundation refused the dorctor's simple request, and killed her husband for no good reason(seems to be). I know the O5 may have their reasons, but it looks like they just kill the man to show they won't be threated by his wife. This reminds me of the boat couple kiled by GOC.

(btw, I'M NOT AN ENGLISH SPEAKER,SORRY)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it weird that I have no idea what you're trying to say? D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whatever, upvote for the idea of telepathy phonecall(THAT'S THE POINT)

AND, the part that MTF killed her husband. oh my, NO PRISONERS.

'Wanna threat us? GOOD. Foundation would like to satisfy you-By sending your love husband to the afterlife. Come on, you said you miss him so much, right?"

The O5 coucil is always warm-hearted like this. Not kidding. It's really…awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks mate!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dialogue felt very hammy and unbelievable, but I really did like the way the concept of the phone app went so much deeper. I feel like there is a lot of potential here for an excellent SCP. You've got a really nice story conceptually here, but it's bogged down by the bad dialogue and kind of a predictable last log. I think a way to improve this would be adjusting the dialogue when she's asking to see her husband, and maybe adding an interview with the husband himself. Getting both sides of that would be very interesting, especially considering the detail that he's an atheist. Maybe a final log of whether or not he was able to join up with his wife, to make it more conclusive. Overall, downvoted for now but I would love to upvote it if it could be improved upon. There's a lot to like here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your thoughtful critique. I will get on these points - especially the "Goodnight Sweetheart" bit because that's a little too convenient for the storyline. I meant for MTF Omicron-0 to be violent, clandestine emergency operations when the Foundation just doesn't have the time for ethics. Their specialty is assassination.

And to be honest, I wasn't trying to piss on SCP-2718, and the whole "sent back" thing in PC-06 mentions it too. It's what happens if you fail or pre-fail the "final exam." I actually contacted the author of 2718, Michael Atreus, to see if he was ok with this article conflicting with 2718, but he was extremely nice about it. Hell, he even upvoted. HE LOOKED ME STRAIGHT IN THE EYE! He was scanning the horizon I AM AWAITED IN VALHALLA

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, I'm doing something I don't like and skipping the rest of the comments, since this has apparently changed a lot.

AS OF NOW:

+ show block
– hide block

This article… ok, I like it. I like the story. Narratively, it gave me whiplash on multiple occasions. It went through a pattern something like this:
Excited reading -> Huge cracking reveal -> Poorly handled transition, repeat.

Examples:

After a six-hour meeting, it was determined by the O5 council that SCP-2922-B had indeed made contact from an extradimensional realm, hereafter SCP-2922-C, and Project Corbenic was started to use SCP-2922-B to explore and ascertain the true nature of SCP-2922-C.

I have absolutely done this myself, and am thus a hypocrite, but in this case that is cramming way too much exposition into way too short a space. I like the image of a six-hour O5 meeting, but designating and founding Corbenic in the same sentence… I'm not saying it needs a ton more content, but that's a really hard transition.

SCP-2922-B: A wagon came to the human camp, driven by a guy in a white robe and a skeletal horse. Says we're being taken to the "Elysian Fields." Paradise, basically.

So now we're confirmed in the legit mythical afterlife?

All it took was for me to tell you of a field of reeds where everyone's happy forever, and you wanted more.

Shit, was she lying about the stuff above? Dramatic and interesting? Yes! Still, super-fast transition, and then we're definitely in Foundation-confirmed hard-mythical/fantasy-afterlife territory. I'm not saying I didn't enjoy that, but man that happened fast.

By the way, if you looked into what happened to O5-11 on that island…

I will admit that I totally thought of SCP-2718 when we started straying into confirmed-afterlife territory. It's weird to see that disconnect not only addressed but basically… flippantly dismissed? Half-coopted? Pissed on? I'm glad you didn't spend more than a sentence on it because that would've been hard to take seriously, but boy was that a statement!

MTF Omicron-0 "Goodnight Sweetheart"

Is this an MTF dedicated to killing the loved ones of Foundation employees? Because if not, that is a weird nickname.

Ok, so, all of that was mostly an attempt to get across my feelings during the article, and why I found it so jarring. Take that as you will, but don't take it as a suggestion to rewrite all of that stuff. Actual suggestions for improvement:

+ show block
– hide block

- Inoculate is totally the wrong technical term, unless the lack of human-telephone-telepathy is considered a disease. I mean, even in-universe people are inoculated against harmful memes.
- I do think the transition at the founding of Project Corbenic was not well-handled. It's… kind of trying to cram enough story into literally a single sentence to slide us from "Dead researcher call" to "Foundation endorsement of afterlife", and it just doesn't accomplish that satisfyingly enough to get over the twist.
- Minor point, but, again, "Goodnight Sweetheart"? You don't need to change it, and it certainly fits the overblown tone of the rest of the article, but still. Weird.


I'm going to upvote because I enjoyed the crazy ride as much as I've enjoyed many tale-like SCPs (including 2718), but I do think the above two bigger issues are significant.

Edit: Also, for the record, I have no quarrel with the current dialogue, and quite like much of it. I especially like the sentence ending in "cowards." It's neat someone bitingly putting metaphysics in Foundation-familiar terms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I gotta say, I love when an article I make generates this much controversy on both ends. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No harm done. Protip: click a user's name to see a Wikidot summary of their profile, like their about, join date, gender etc. If their gender/pronouns aren't there, probably best to use they or them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I'm sorry - I really didn't know. :(

I'll edit my previous post, so I don't come off as an offensive person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, the article just told us what happened when someone inoculated with the phone number died. While the Foundation can't know it exactly up front, it was probably better they didn't have this happen with a D-Class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's only one phone number this can call, right? They can just terminate the guy if he dicks around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You do realize that Decibelles is a woman, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An excellent point Lady Decibelles.
I completely agree.
That hypen would make the sentence much easier to read. (At least for me, but I'm glad I'm not alone on this one - since I'm not a native speaker I really thought I missed some word play here and I read the sentence ten times over) :)

*Edit: Did not address the user properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tone of this article needs a lot of work. It's really awkward. Also the dialogue isn't really believable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a lot better. I especially love the implications of

Dr. Janet Spiegel, advisor to [DATA EXPUNGED] the Impenetrable, signing off.

There's a couple issues though.

So you can waive the difficult training phase and skip to important mission in your existence, without a clue of how to win?

This sentence sounds like nothing a person would say, at least in my opinion.

sidenote :

and you can be here with me with you follow my path.

I assume the end was meant to be "if you follow my path"

Anyhow, for the final speech, I think one of the prior version (that already mentioned the [DATA EXPUNGED] the impenetrable but otherwise was similar to the original) worked better, for the sole reason it sounded more like something someone might rattle off in a hurry, which is what I imagine happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Major revision of the two speeches at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Presumably, the sky takes on a sea-green color, and the clouds are black. If that's the case, there should be a hyphen in-between sea and green in all the cleanup that happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sea green sky, black clouds.

The comma confuses me here. (Well, to be honest the whole use of punctuation/wording in this sentence… considering this was before she went insane. )

Is this intended and I am just not getting the word play here?

Nevertheless - an excellent SCP. Congratulations!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because a D-class would totes use it to make prank calls.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't say I'm terribly interested in the story this tells; Decibelles is right that it feels more like a tale. And I just have to wonder why they tested it on a Doctor when D-class…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little disappointed the narmy "Is it Heaven or Hell?" part is still there. Given all the crazy stuff the Foundation's seen, limiting it to those choices seems a bit… naive.

Also,

If the scarlet font of seeing of [DATA EXPUNGED]

That's a lot of uses of of. Probably not the highest priority to work on, but I had to roll that sentence around in my head for longer than I'd like to parse it, even before trying to glean some meaning for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno. You have removed a good few bits which seemed fine to me, while leaving in possibly the corniest two lines the story had.

Dr. ██████: Alright, just stay calm, get back to us when you can. But just tell me one thing. Where you are - is it Heaven or Hell?

SCP-2922-B: I'm gonna go with "neither" for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made a serious pass at de-hamming the dialogue. Any suggestions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
██████ Corporation

Some crazy stuff coming out of that Google X lab, huh?

Since you've said you're going to rework it, I'm going to hold off on rating for now. Looking forward to seeing what you can do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Inoculate" also means to transfer a microbial culture to another medium (as in both lab work "inoculate a petri dish" and baking "use a starter culture to create four cups of sourdough inoculum"). Extended to memetics it could mean to transfer a meme to a new carrier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once a human is inoculated with SCP-2922….

You've used the word "inoculated" several times, but I suspect that you mean something else (though I can't figure out what). To "inoculate" someone means to render them immune to a disease. For instance, you can be inoculated against the flu with a vaccine.

Does SCP-2922 protect you against some disease or condition? If not, I think you should find another word for what you mean.

SCP-2922 is a method of communication from a human mind to a telephone.

SCP-2922 was developed by the ██████ Corporation, initially as a novelty smartphone app.

So is SCP-2922 the app, or is it the underlying method of communication? How does someone gain the ability to communicate with phones telepathically? By using the smartphone app? If so, then the app probably needs a letter designation that is separate from the telepathic ability. If not, then how does one develop the ability to use SCP-2922?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot of the notes I've been getting have to do with dialogue. Tell you what - I'm gonna spend a couple days making nitpicky edits to the dialogue to remove a great deal of melodrama. Thanks for letting me know guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dialogue is a weak part of this article. These are the places where I cringed to read it, because it was readily apparent that I was reading someone pretending to be a female middle-aged scientist. And when your article relies entirely on building a character and making them feel authentic, this is a huge problem.

This, pretty much, is my problem with the article, though, I'd say it gets slightly better later on (I wouldn't even include the last line on the list, possibly) - it's mainly the first log where he fails to communicate some sort of expected reaction, or fails to build ground for an unexpected one.

Which is a shame because I love the rest of this, especially the encounter with a few kilometres tall primate.

As a final verdict, no vote, at least not yet.

EDIT:

Hmm, the more I think about it, the more I feel that the precise reason they have refused to let her call with her husband is that higher up, what she states is not thought as an accurate representation of afterlife, or in fact anything at all beyond a remainder of her mind kept somewhere and living out its own bizarre concepts in self-generated dreamworld. Which is a part of the reason why exposure to her was thought unhealthy, for him at least.

EDIT2: Upon further pontification, i actually see no statement that'd imply the researcher is middle-aged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have numerous issues with this. There's little things like the O5s having a huddle and then suddenly coming to the conclusion that they're receiving a call from Another Dimension(tm). The Foundation making the monumentally stupid decision to not just humor the researcher for the purposes of gathering information and bring her husband to talk to her for reasons. The clumsy lampshading of SCP-2718. But my main problem is this:

So, out of all the ways I could die in this stupid-ass Foundation, it had to be a non-anomalous car crash.

As soon as I see something other than sand and melancholy, I'll contact you again.

it helps when you have an omniscient demon god on your side

This is Janet, I'm dead, and I still love the living shit out of you.

Dialogue is a weak part of this article. These are the places where I cringed to read it, because it was readily apparent that I was reading someone pretending to be a female middle-aged scientist. And when your article relies entirely on building a character and making them feel authentic, this is a huge problem. This does not sound like someone who has just probably had their entire worldview destroyed and is in an incomprehensible crazy afterlife dimension of some sort. This sounds like a smart-ass teenager that's been put out by some sort of inconvenience.

The inevitable rejoinder to this is always "well there are people who really do talk like that." And yes, I suppose that's true. I too have met the scientist who speaks like a 4chan thread. Thing is, this is kind of an outlier from what I would expect a character like the one you're portraying to speak like, and and it is entirely too close to how, say, a twentysomething dude who hangs out on an internet writing community sounds like. You require authenticity for this to make any sort of impression, and in my reading, this is lacking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really have been missing stuff like this, reminds me of some of the earlier works. I do have to admit I am a sucker for cross-references, and I think this one is subtle enough to pull it off without being blatant and frustrating. The characters are good, even though the dialogue is a bit shaky at times, and the story was odd enough to get me thinking. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good lord, this is the most epic thing. I love her directions at the end - it reminds me of 'ritual pastas' as the category is called on Creepypasta in a very good way - and it gives me this awesome sense of adventure. This is my kind of sci-fi/fantasy right here, and it's well-written. Viciously upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very intelligent critique, I must say. Made my day to be honest. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hrm.

Gonna keep my downvote. It doesn't do enough to keep me interested. Feels like a combo of Video Oddity and that one Stephen King short story.
